data.title: Air Force, Navy plan to boost purchases of Lockheed F-35 jets over five years
data.description:
The U.S. Air Force and Navy proposed stepping up purchases of Lockheed Martin's (LMT) F-35 fighter jet over the next five years, a show of confidence for a plane that critics have said is becoming obsolete, Bloomberg reported Friday.

Planned purchases of the Air Force model would rise steadily from 38 jets in FY 2027 to 42 in 2028, 46 in 2029, and 48 in both 2030 and 2031, while purchases by the Navy and Marine Corps would jump to 47 jets next year from this year’s 23, before dropping to 43 in 2028 and 38 or fewer each year during 2029-31, according to new projections.

The increased quantities are part of the Trump administration's request to Congress to increase the defense budget by more than 40% to $1.5T, which is bound to meet resistance from both Democrats and fiscal conservatives.

The planned purchases would be good news for Lockheed (LMT), with the total number of F-35s requested up to 85 jets from the 47 Congress approved for this year.

The projections failed to improve the performance of Lockheed's (LMT) stock, which has declined for nine straight sessions—slumping 17.1% during the period—and fell another 3.1% on Friday to finish at $513.45, its lowest close since January 7.

data.title: First Business targets 10% annual loan and core deposit growth for 2026 as CEO transition nears
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Business Financial Services (FBIZ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Corey Chambas said the company delivered “a strong start to 2026,” adding that it “won new relationships in a highly competitive environment, growing loans and deposits at a pace that well exceeded our expectations,” and that fee income grew “nearly 16% year-over-year” with Private Wealth posting “record revenues.” (CEO & Director Corey Chambas)
  • Chambas framed the quarter around profitability and capital, stating, “we grew net income and earnings per share by more than 9% over last year's first quarter,” and “our strong earnings and disciplined capital deployment drove 14% year-over-year growth in tangible book value per share.” (CEO & Director Corey Chambas)
  • President David Seiler emphasized the growth mix and timing: “In the first quarter, we grew loans by $126 million or 15%,” with “$90 million or 72% in March,” and said pipelines are “lighter going into Q2” with “some known payoffs,” leading to an expectation that “the second quarter [will] be lighter on growth than Q1” while remaining “on track to achieve our 10% annual growth goal for 2026.” (President & COO David Seiler)
  • Seiler highlighted deposits and fee income, saying “first quarter balances were up 18% from the linked quarter and up 14% year-over-year,” and Private Wealth produced “record revenue of $3.9 million, up 11% year-over-year,” representing “more than 40% of our total quarterly fee income.” (President & COO Seiler)
  • CFO Brian Spielmann said margin results reflected quarter mechanics and growth timing: “First quarter net interest margin increased 3 basis points to 3.56%,” and “excluding” fewer accrual days, “first quarter NIM was 3.61%,” while the adjusted Q4 comparison would have been “3.63%.” (CFO & Treasurer Brian Spielmann)
  • Seiler disclosed a leadership change and performance context: “this is Corey's last call before his retirement next week,” and added the company will be “very happy that Corey will be continuing to serve on our Board.” (President & COO Seiler)

Outlook

  • Spielmann guided to the annual net interest margin framework: “we think the early momentum of C&I loan growth in Q1 positions us well to operate within or toward the lower to middle portion of our targeted 3.60% to 3.65% range for the year,” assuming “a stable to modestly changing interest rate environment.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • Management reiterated growth and fee objectives: Seiler said the company targets “10% loan and core deposit growth on an annual basis,” while Spielmann stated, “Our strong first quarter fee income supports our expectation of 10% growth for the full year compared to 2025.” (President & COO Seiler; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • Seiler flagged near-term moderation after Q1 pull-forward: “After an extremely strong first quarter, our pipelines are lighter going into Q2,” and “we will have some known payoffs in the second quarter,” with “normalization in the second half of the year.” (President & COO Seiler)
  • No management EPS or revenue guidance was provided in the prepared remarks or Q&A in this transcript.

Financial Results

  • Spielmann reported net interest margin details and drivers, including “3.56%” reported NIM and “3.61%” excluding fewer accrual days, and said the adjusted change “primarily reflects the late quarter timing of loan growth.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • On fees and expense cadence, Spielmann stated Q4 comparisons were affected by “an accounting classification change,” and reiterated Q1 fee strength as a “good starting point for quarterly fee income in 2026,” while acknowledging compensation seasonality: “Compensation expense increased by about $1.4 million” from Q4, and FTEs were “up about 5.7% from a year ago.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • On taxes and operating leverage, Spielmann said, “The effective tax rate was 15.2% for the first quarter,” and the company expects “16% to 18% for 2026,” while reiterating an expense aim of “annual positive operating leverage.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: asked whether long-term profitability targets on Slide 15 might change under Seiler’s leadership; President & COO Seiler responded, “every quarter or more often, we look at all of these metrics,” and “at this point, we still think these are good metrics for us to be working on,” while noting the efficiency ratio “blipped up a little bit this quarter” and is expected “to kind of return.”
  • Tamayo, Raymond James: asked how to model seasonality and the margin path after late-quarter loan growth; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said a Q1 day-count impact is typical but “it was more of a timing difference,” and Seiler confirmed margin expectations: “the margin comes back up into the range in the second quarter and then relatively stable from there? David Seiler: Yes.”
  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson: pressed on whether Q1 expenses are an appropriate run rate; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann agreed, “Exactly. Yes, spot on,” aligning to a high-single-digit annualized view implied by the question.
  • Rulis, D.A. Davidson: asked for the mix of loan growth between new vs. existing clients; President & COO Seiler said growth “was spread fairly evenly,” and CEO & Director Chambas added, “always our growth is going to be driven by new.”
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked about fee-income momentum and SBA; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann reiterated “10% year-over-year growth expectations,” and Seiler said SBA was expected “to be a little bit higher this quarter” but pipelines suggest it will be “relatively flat going forward.”
  • Race, Piper Sandler: asked about Q2 growth magnitude; Seiler responded, “I think that's probably reasonable for Q2,” citing payoffs “in flux,” while reiterating “we still expect to be at 10% for the year.”
  • Race, Piper Sandler: asked about charge-offs and workout timing; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said charge-offs were “about 25 basis points” in Q1 versus “around 20 basis points on average for the year,” while Seiler said on the CRE NPA, “we are pursuing foreclosure,” with timing “more the back half of the year,” and on the ABL credit, “an end of the year type event most likely.”
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: asked about fees-in-lieu and provisioning; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said fees-in-lieu were “about $2.2 million,” and on provisioning, “provisioning for growth off this reserve level with the 20 basis points, I think that's appropriate,” adding the macro outlook is “the wildcard.”
  • Brian Martin, Brean Capital: asked where loan growth is most likely and whether hiring continues; Seiler pointed to “ABL,” “accounts receivable finance,” “Kansas City,” and “Southern Wisconsin markets,” and said another Kansas City hire is possible: “we could have another add.”
  • Martin, Brean Capital: asked about fee-income lumpiness; CFO & Treasurer Spielmann said “there's still going to be lumpiness,” citing “swap fees and SBA gains,” while describing efforts to increase stability through Private Wealth, service charges, and increased SBIC fund deployment within a “5% limit.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive, with repeated congratulations and a focus on potential pressure points (expense run-rate, Q2 growth deceleration, margin modeling, provisioning, and credit timelines), including Rulis stating expenses “seemed a little high.”
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly using expectation-setting around normalization and variability, including Seiler’s “pipelines are lighter going into Q2” and Spielmann’s “there's still going to be lumpiness.”
  • Versus last quarter, the dialogue shifted from dissecting a new nonaccrual downgrade and its NIM impact to execution on growth and deposit gathering, while still returning to credit-resolution timelines and provisioning assumptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The most material new development was leadership: Q1 included Seiler’s statement that it was “Corey's last call before his retirement next week,” with Chambas staying on the Board, whereas Q4 did not include a CEO transition timeline.
  • Guidance language tightened around near-term growth pacing: Q4 emphasized pipelines “look strong for the first quarter” and an expectation that growth would “rebound,” while Q1 stressed Q2 moderation due to “pull-forward” growth and “known payoffs,” followed by “normalization in the second half of the year.”
  • Credit discussion evolved from identifying and contextualizing the $20.4 million CRE downgrade in Q4 to reporting Q1 actions, including a “$3.4 million” land loan sale “at par,” and setting expectations that further progress is more likely “before the second half of 2026.”
  • Margin framing moved from Q4’s reported compression tied to the nonaccrual interest reversal to Q1’s emphasis on timing noise and returning toward the “3.60% to 3.65%” target range.

Risks and Concerns

  • Seiler cited “the same wildcard as everyone else” and said management will “continue to monitor for any impact of oil prices and geopolitical uncertainty.” (President & COO Seiler)
  • Management highlighted competitive pressures while emphasizing discipline: Spielmann noted a “competitive environment” for deposit pricing and described using wholesale funding “to match-fund fixed rate loans and maintain NIM stability.” (CFO & Treasurer Spielmann)
  • Credit resolution timing remains uncertain: Seiler said the remaining CRE nonperforming portfolio resolution timing “will be variable,” with foreclosure proceedings implying limited progress before the second half of 2026, and the ABL case moving “very, very slowly” through court. (President & COO Seiler)

Final Takeaway

Corey Chambas described Q1 as a “strong start to 2026,” led by outsized loan growth and double-digit core deposit growth, while David Seiler cautioned that Q2 pipelines are “lighter” with “known payoffs” after growth was pulled into March. Brian Spielmann maintained the company’s annual framework of a 3.60% to 3.65% net interest margin and 10% fee-income growth, while acknowledging quarterly noise, expense seasonality, and continued lumpiness in certain fee lines. Management also highlighted measurable progress on the previously downgraded CRE relationship through a $3.4 million sale at par, but guided that additional resolution is likely weighted to the back half of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Rexford outlines $400M-$500M dispositions as it raises 2026 core FFO per share midpoint by $0.02
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The Rexford team delivered a strong quarter" and "set a record for leasing activity, executing 4.1 million square feet of leases," CEO & Director Laura Clark said, adding that "the decisive actions we are taking to advance our strategic priorities are driving top- and bottom-line growth" (CEO & Director Laura Clark).
  • Clark said the company has "closed on $144 million of dispositions with another $170 million under contract or accepted offer," framing the plan as "derisking cash flows" and "reducing development exposure" while supporting "accretive capital recycling" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Clark emphasized that "share repurchases remain a compelling driver of FFO and NAV per share accretion," and said, "In the first quarter, we executed $200 million of share repurchases" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Clark pointed to tenant-demand indicators, including that "current leasing interest on our vacant spaces increased to approximately 90% compared to 75% last quarter and a year ago," while adding "market fundamentals are still under pressure" in certain areas (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Chief Operating Officer John Nahas said first-quarter leasing was "comprised of 144 deals averaging 29,000 square feet with approximately 70% coming from renewals" and reported "cash re-leasing spreads for the quarter were negative 15.4%... and negative 1.8%, excluding the Tireco renewal" (Chief Operating Officer John Nahas).
  • CFO Michael Fitzmaurice said Q1 "Core FFO per share of $0.61" was "up $0.02 sequentially" and added, "We ended the quarter with net debt to adjusted EBITDA of 4.5x and $1.3 billion of total liquidity with no significant maturities until 2027" (Chief Financial Officer Michael Fitzmaurice).

Outlook

  • Fitzmaurice said, "We are raising our full year Core FFO per share midpoint by $0.02" and also "raised our same-property NOI growth outlook by 50 basis points at the midpoint" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • He said average same-property occupancy is now expected to be "95.1% to 95.6%, up 30 basis points at the midpoint," while the "bad debt assumption of 75 basis points of revenue remains unchanged" and "net effective re-leasing spreads of 5% to 10%" also remain unchanged (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • Fitzmaurice kept other guideposts intact, including "G&A of approximately $60 million" and "interest expense of approximately $112 million" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • On value creation, he said the company expects to "stabilize and commence rent on approximately 1.1 million square feet of value-added projects, generating $17 million of annualized NOI" and reiterated "approximately $12 million of annualized in-place NOI will come offline related to 2026 construction starts" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Financial Results

  • Fitzmaurice attributed Q1 performance to "stronger NOI growth and accretive share buybacks" and said sequential improvement was driven "primarily by lower G&A" along with buybacks and NOI (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • He reported same-property NOI growth was "90 basis points on a net effective basis and negative 40 basis points on cash," and said "bad debt... was elevated this quarter" but "concentrated in a few tenants and not broad-based" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • Fitzmaurice said buybacks were funded by dispositions: "Disposition proceeds were redeployed into share buybacks" and "we bought back $200 million of shares at a weighted average price of $36" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Q&A

  • Craig Mailman, Citi: asked where leasing strength is showing up; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said demand themes included "construction-related uses, advanced manufacturing... food and beverage" and added that for Class A, "there are exceptions" including "South Bay" and pockets of "San Diego" and "Long Beach" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Samir Khanal, BofA: asked to reconcile improving activity vs. slower development leasing; CEO & Director Clark said "we are encouraged by the early signs of improvement" but "market fundamentals are under pressure" with "net absorption... negative" and "vacancy ticked up" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank: asked who is buying dispositions and cap rates; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said user sales produced "pretty strong cap rates" and "on a blended basis, we were below 4% this quarter" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Michael Griffin, Evercore: asked where market rents are and spread guidance context; CFO Fitzmaurice said re-leasing expectations are "between 5% and 10%" net effective and "flat to negative 5%" cash, adding "we do expect re-leasing spreads to reaccelerate through the back half of this year" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • Michael Mueller, JPMorgan: asked if buybacks require more dispositions; CFO Fitzmaurice said, "buybacks are tied to disposition activity" and reiterated disposition expectations "between $400 million and $500 million" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).
  • John Kim, BMO: asked whether buybacks could be paused if the stock reaction stays muted; CEO & Director Clark said the company will "continue to assess" capital allocation for "the highest risk-adjusted returns" and pointed again to the "disconnect" between intrinsic value and trading price (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Vince Tibone, Green Street: asked if record leasing is driven by renewals and whether Rexford is pushing early renewals; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said "we're prioritizing" renewals as part of "our overall strategy to prioritize occupancy" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho: asked for leasing/commencement square footage targets and whether Rexford would consider a much larger portfolio sale; CFO Fitzmaurice said guidance assumes rent commencement of "between 8 million and 8.5 million this year," and CEO & Director Clark said dispositions remain focused on recycling "on an accretive basis" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice; CEO & Director Clark).
  • Richard Anderson, Cantor: asked about demand linkage from advanced manufacturing/data centers; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said "data centers is not really a core component" but for advanced manufacturing "the answer is yes... a very bold, connected line" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Nicholas Thillman, Baird: asked about shorter lease terms; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said Rexford may "proactively try to shorten terms strategically" depending on leverage and that smaller units "tend to have shorter terms anyway" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: asked the long-term plan for the Tireco asset; Chief Operating Officer Nahas said, "it's not really a read-through to any longer-term strategic plan for that asset" (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Young Ku, Wells Fargo: asked whether pro forma redevelopment rents imply embedded growth; CFO Fitzmaurice said, "No, that has to do with the mix issue" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical around the durability of improving demand and the payoff from buybacks, including questions that "reconcile" improving fundamentals with "still taking a bit longer" on development leasing and whether buybacks would be "pausing" if the dynamic continues (Samir Khanal, BofA; John Kim, BMO).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly pairing encouragement with caveats such as "we are encouraged by the early positive signs" while also stating "market fundamentals are still under pressure" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Versus last quarter’s repeated framing that the company was "not yet able to call an inflection point," this quarter management emphasized "incremental improvement" and pointed to leading indicators like "leasing interest" rising to "approximately 90%" (CEO & Director Clark).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 highlighted execution and raised guidance, with Fitzmaurice saying the company is "raising" the Core FFO midpoint by "$0.02," versus Q4 introducing 2026 guidance of "$2.35 to $2.40" and emphasizing "near-term pressure impacting our 2026 growth expectations" (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice; CEO & Director Laura Clark).
  • Disposition and capital-recycling themes continued, but Q1 added a larger disclosed quarter buyback pace ("$200 million") and a clearer linkage that "Disposition proceeds were redeployed into share buybacks," while Q4 framed repurchases as a consideration "subject to" discount and capital needs (Chief Financial Officer Michael Fitzmaurice).
  • Analysts remained focused across both calls on Tireco, leasing spreads, occupancy, and the buyer pool for dispositions; in Q1, questions broadened to whether the market is "bottoming" and whether buybacks are being "rewarded" (Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank; John Kim, BMO).

Risks and Concerns

  • Clark said "market fundamentals are still under pressure" and tied stabilization to needing "net absorption turning positive... vacancy moving down and rates firming" (CEO & Director Clark).
  • Nahas reported the market context remained challenging: "negative net absorption" and "rents declining approximately 70 basis points compared to last quarter," while noting Class A demand in some submarkets remains slow and has led to "delayed rent commencement" on delivered projects (Chief Operating Officer Nahas).
  • Fitzmaurice acknowledged "near-term pressure from re-leasing spreads" driven by "market rent decline over the past 3 years" and positioned mitigation around occupancy focus, the value-creation pipeline, and capital recycling (Chief Financial Officer Fitzmaurice).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a record leasing quarter alongside an active capital-recycling program, including $144 million of closed dispositions, $170 million under contract or accepted offer, and $200 million of buybacks. The company raised full-year Core FFO midpoint guidance by $0.02 and increased same-property NOI growth outlook, while continuing to flag pressure from negative net absorption, soft pockets in demand (notably some Class A segments), elevated but concentrated bad debt, and near-term re-leasing spread headwinds that management expects to improve in the back half of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: J&J's Xarelto, Invokana to become available through TrumpRx
data.description:
• Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) will make its blood thinner Xarelto (rivaroxaban) and type 2 diabetes medicine Invokana (canagliflozin) available for purchase through the TrumpRx website. 
  • The drug giant will also offer the diabetes drugs metformin and metformin extended-release, per a CBS News report.
  • J&J, like other pharmaceutical companies, previously agreed to work with the administration to provide some of its drugs discounted and build new plants in exchange for not having its imported drugs subject to tariffs.
  • In February, the company said it would spend $1B to build a cell therapy manufacturing facility in Pennsylvania.

data.title: Gold turns higher after DoJ ends Powell probe, still lower for the week
data.description:
Gold and silver futures edged higher on Friday following news that the U.S. Department of Justice dropped its criminal investigation of outgoing Fed Chair Jerome Powell, paving the way for Kevin Warsh to step in as Powell's successor, which in turn has sparked some optimism for lower interest rates sooner than later.

Senator Tillis, a Republican and Banking Committee member, had effectively prevented the full Senate from voting to confirm Warsh as Fed chair unless the investigation ended.

U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro, the top federal prosecutor for the District of Columbia, said the Federal Reserve's inspector general was asked to investigate cost overruns in the multibillion-dollar renovation of the Fed's headquarters in Washington.

Powell and others had said that the real reason for the DoJ probe was to pressure him and the Fed to lower interest rates as President Trump wanted.

Last summer, Trump sought to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook, who, like Powell, had resisted his demands to cut rates.

Gold prices fell for the full week as stalled talks between the U.S. and Iran lifted oil prices, raising fears of inflation and expectations that central banks would keep interest rates higher for longer.

Benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury yields jumped 1.5% this week, raising ⁠the opportunity ​cost of holding gold, while the dollar posted its first weekly gain in three, making bullion more expensive for holders of other currencies.

"The longer the Strait [of Hormuz] remains shut, the greater the oil shock and the more distant the idea of Brent crude trading back to levels near $80 or below," ING analysts said in a note. "This view is very much dominant in the interest rate market, where short-dated yields remain very firm on the view that many central banks will have to react to this inflationary shock."

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery closed up 0.4% to $4,722.30/oz, and front-month Comex silver (XAGUSD:CUR) for April delivery gained 1.2% to $76.383/oz.

For the full week, gold and silver fell 2.8% and 5.5%, respectively.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (DUST), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Rivalry announces board, management departures
data.description:
• Rivalry (RVLY:CA) on Friday said several directors and executives have stepped down as part of an operational transition.
  • Stephen Rigby, Steven Isenberg, Ryan White, and Kevin Wimer resigned from the board, effective immediately.
  • White also stepped down as Chief Technology Officer, and Wimer as Chief Operating Officer.
  • Separately, Demi Abidogun-Benson resigned as interim Chief Financial Officer.
  • The company said it would provide further updates as appropriate.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Bgin Blockchain Limited GAAP EPS of -$1.62, revenue of $67.4M
data.description:
• Bgin Blockchain Limited press release (BGIN): FY GAAP EPS of -$1.62.
  • Revenue of $67.4M (-77.7% Y/Y).
  • As of December 31, 2025, the Company had US$26.3 million in cash, compared to US$114.8 million as of December 31, 2024.

data.title: ADT hit by cloud breach: Limited data exposed, probe underway
data.description:
• ADT (ADT) announced on Friday via a Form 8-K filing that it detected unauthorized access in part of its cloud systems and quickly shut it down.
  • The company said only a limited amount of customer and potential customer data was accessed, and experts have been brought in to check the issue.
  • ADT believes this will not hurt its business or financials in a big way, but it is still looking into the full details.
  • Following the news, the stock traded slightly lower at ~$7.19 in after-hours.

data.title: S&P 500 hits another record high as U.S. continues ceasefire negotiations with Iran
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages ended mixed on Friday, but the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq achieved another record close as investors evaluated the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension of the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) closed up 0.8% to another record high of $7,165.08 points, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite gained 1.64%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was down 0.16%.

On a week-to-date basis, the benchmark S&P 500 is up 0.55%.

Seeking Alpha analyst Agar Capital said that the key takeaway from the tape is not that investors are simply disregarding geopolitical issues, but rather that investors now believe there is a greater chance of a “containment” type of outcome.

The analyst added that a 0.55% weekly increase in the S&P 500 shows investors' willingness to continue to buy into the index; however, the fact that we are seeing such narrow breadth across many sectors indicates to me that this is still more of an "on the edge" risk-on situation versus an outright, all-in re-positioning.

On a sector-by-sector basis, five of the 11 S&P segments closed in the green. Technology (XLK) and Energy (XLE) were the strongest performers this week, with gains of 3.84% and 3.4%, respectively.

Agar Capital argued that investors' rapid transition back to focusing on earnings (and specifically investing in artificial intelligence) based on reduced geopolitical uncertainty, clearly illustrated through Technology's leadership within this rally, also indicates that many investors believe the worst of the current situation may be behind us and demonstrates investors' willingness to return to issues that were left pending a few months ago. The war continues, negotiations have not yet led to a happy ending, but meanwhile, tech companies are still making profits.

At the other end of the spectrum, Healthcare (XLV) and Communication Services (XLC) were the biggest detractors this week, with losses of 3.07% and 2.91%, respectively.

According to the Seeking Alpha analyst, weakness in Healthcare appears to be driven more from a rotation away from defensive stocks and specific pressure on large-cap Pharma stocks, as opposed to being indicative of a complete reset in fundamentals. News regarding CVS’ decision to no longer cover anti-obesity medications under Medicare created downward pressure on Eli Lilly, reminding investors that strong growth profiles for healthcare companies continue to be susceptible to changes in policy and reimbursement. This has clearly impacted the entire sector.

Additionally, "The decline in Communication Services was also largely a position trade and not driven by fundamentals. To me, investors took advantage of some mega-cap Internet/media exposure to fund their trades and rotated into Technology and Energy."

Sectors’ weekly performance breakdown: 

Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK): +3.84%

Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE): +3.4%

Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP): +0.94%

Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLB): +0.13%

Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU): +0.09%

Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI): -0.56%

Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY): -1.40%

Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE): -1.42%

Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF): -1.81%

Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLC): -2.91%

Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV): -3.07%

data.title: Park National GAAP EPS of $2.39 misses by $0.21, revenue of $159.5M beats by $2.43M
data.description:
• Park National press release (PRK): Q1 GAAP EPS of $2.39 misses by $0.21.
  • Revenue of $159.5M (+22.6% Y/Y) beats by $2.43M.
